e-Edition

Trending:

American track and field talent Nikki Hiltz was intent on medaling at the 2024 Summer Olympics in Paris. The Aptos native fell short of that goal, but still produced an inspirational and historic showing.

The 29-year-old, who identifies as transgender/nonbinary and uses they/them pronouns, became the first openly trans athlete to compete in an Olympic final in an individual event.

The Aptos native placed seventh in the women’s 1,500 final in 3 minutes, 56.38 seconds on Saturday, the penultimate day of the Paris Games.

“To measure my Olympic experience off of less than a 4-minute race would be such a disservice to this whole journey,” Hiltz said. “I’ve had such an incredible time and really taking it all in, walking away very happy.”

Hiltz had plenty of reasons to be proud of their showing. They had just one sub-4 finish in the 1,500 prior to 2024 but continued to show their potential. They’ve posted finishes of 3:56 or faster three times this year, including twice in the past three days.

Hiltz’s parents made the trip from Aptos to the Stade de France in Saint-Denis, France, to cheer on their offspring. That said, Hiltz, a former talent at University of Oregon and later University of Arkansas, has support groups around the world. Hiltz an inspiration in the LGBTQ community.

Millions of fans were glued to their televisions to take in the 1,500 final. Some of them went to Zelda’s on the Beach in Capitola Village for a watch party to support Hiltz.

What those restaurant viewers witnessed, as did the rest of the world, was a historic showing from Kenyan 30-year-old Faith Kipyegon.



Kipyegon ran the race of her life and claimed her third straight gold in an Olympic-record 3:51.29. She broke her own mark of 3:53.11, set at the Tokyo Games in 2021.

She is the first athlete — male or female — to win gold in the event at three different Games.

“It’s a big, big achievement,” Kipyegon said. “I was really looking forward to defending my title (from the last two Olympic Games), and I had a dream. It’s amazing to me, I completed it. I’m so, so happy.

“This is history. I managed to make history. I’ve done it. This is an amazing honor, to win the gold medal in the 1,500. It was my key target.”

Australia’s Jessica Hull won silver in 3:52.56, and Great Britain’s Georgia Bell earned bronze in a national record time of 3:52.61.

“It’s all a bit of a blur,” Bell said. “I think it’ll hit me a little bit later. On the start (line) I really had the feeling that if I just stuck in there, I’d be able to do something special, so I’m over the moon to get a medal.”

Hiltz, possessing a big, warm smile, helped an exhausted runner up from the track after the race and congratulated several runners on their showing, including the medalists.

Hiltz, who smashed the U.S. Olympic Trials record in a 3:55.33 finish last month in Oregon, took third place in both the first round and semifinals in France.

Their success has skyrocketed so high over the past two years that Hiltz envisioned medaling in her first Olympics appearance.

“Why not me?” said Hiltz, after the semifinals.

The stars didn’t align. Hiltz got boxed in early and drafted the lead pack for much of the first three laps. Hiltz got tangled up with another runner just before the bell sounding the final lap was rung. They maintained their footing while breaking stride, but they were unable to have their infamous kick secure a podium finish.

Regardless of the outcome, Hiltz’s fans will continue to support their racing hero.

In fact,several thousand of them have signed a petition to General Mills executives in an attempt to get Hiltz featured on their Wheaties cereal box.

It’s an honor typically reserved for champions, and, to them, Hiltz is a champion.

Olympics.com and Citiusmag.com contributed to this report.

Women’s 1,500 meters

1. Faith Kipyegon, Kenya, 3:51.29.

2. Jessica Hull, Australia, 3:52.56.

3. Georgia Bell, Britain, 3:52.61.

4. Diribe Welteji, Ethiopia, 3:52.75.

5. Laura Muir, Britain, 3:53.37.

6. Susan Lokayo Ejore, Kenya, 3:56.07.

7. Nikki Hiltz, United States, 3:56.38.

8. Elle St. Pierre, United States, 3:57.52.

9. Agathe Guillemot, France, 3:59.08.

10. Klaudia Kazimierska, Poland, 4:00.12.

11. Agueda Marques, Spain, 4:00.31.

12. Gudaf Tsegay, Ethiopia, 4:01.27.

title: Aptos native Nikki Hiltz takes seventh in 1,500 | 2024 Summer Olympics – Santa Cruz Sentinel
description: American track and field talent Nikki Hiltz was intent on medaling at the 2024 Summer Olympics in Paris. The Aptos native fell short of that goal, but still produced an inspirational and historic showing.
